content:
Relational **GROUP BY** and **JOIN** semantics come from SQL (1970s relational algebra). Spreadsheet users performed similar operations with **pivot tables** and **VLOOKUP** long before ML pipelines existed.

**pandas** (2008–) brought split-apply-combine to Python scientific stacks, mirroring **Split-Apply-Combine** described by Hadley Wickham in the R **plyr** ecosystem. **`DataFrame.merge`** wraps database-style joins for in-memory tables, replacing ad hoc loops over Python dicts.

As ML teams moved from single CSVs to **multi-table feature pipelines**, groupby summaries and merges became the default way to build **entity-level features** (user totals, product categories). Kaggle competitions repeatedly exposed **leakage via merges**—especially ID columns and post-outcome joins—pushing practitioners toward **split-first** aggregation and later **point-in-time** feature stores.

Modern stacks still use pandas groupby/joins in notebooks and batch jobs, while production systems often compile the same logic to SQL or Spark—but the join-type and leakage rules defined here remain the same.

timeline.label: Relational algebra and SQL joins
timeline.description: Formal inner/outer join semantics established how tables combine on keys—foundation for all later dataframe merge APIs.

timeline.label: Split-apply-combine in R (plyr)
timeline.description: Wickham's pattern influenced pandas groupby design: split by key, apply function, combine results.

timeline.label: pandas merge and groupby APIs
timeline.description: pandas 0.x–1.x standardized merge, join, and named aggregations for Python data science workflows.

timeline.label: ML join-leakage awareness
timeline.description: Competitions and industrial CRM/fraud projects documented global aggregations and label merges as top causes of inflated offline metrics.
